title: Find All Palindrome Pairs
description: Given words array, find pairs (i,j) such that words[i]+words[j] is palindrome. Example: ["bat","tab","cat"] -> (0,1),(1,0)

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Initialize result = []
Step 2: n = 3
Step 3: i=0, j=0: skip (i=j)
Step 4: i=0, j=1: candidate = "bat" + "tab" = "battab"
        Check palindrome: 'b' vs 'b' ✓, 'a' vs 'a' ✓, 't' vs 't' ✓ → palindrome ✓
        Add [0,1] to result → result = [[0,1]]
Step 5: i=0, j=2: candidate = "bat" + "cat" = "batcat"
        Check: 'b' vs 't' ✗ → not palindrome
Step 6: i=1, j=0: candidate = "tab" + "bat" = "tabbat"
        Check: 't' vs 't' ✓, 'a' vs 'a' ✓, 'b' vs 'b' ✓ → palindrome ✓
        Add [1,0] to result → result = [[0,1], [1,0]]
Step 7: i=1, j=1: skip
Step 8: i=1, j=2: candidate = "tab" + "cat" = "tabcat"
        Check: 't' vs 't' ✓, 'a' vs 'a' ✓, 'b' vs 'c' ✗ → not palindrome
Step 9: i=2, j=0: candidate = "cat" + "bat" = "catbat"
        Check: 'c' vs 't' ✗ → not palindrome
Step 10: i=2, j=1: candidate = "cat" + "tab" = "cattab"
         Check: 'c' vs 'b' ✗ → not palindrome
Step 11: i=2, j=2: skip
Final result: [[0,1], [1,0]]

Step 1: result = [], n = 3
Step 2: i=0, j=0: skip
Step 3: i=0, j=1: candidate = "a" + "b" = "ab"
        Check: 'a' vs 'b' ✗ → not palindrome
Step 4: i=0, j=2: candidate = "a" + "a" = "aa"
        Check: 'a' vs 'a' ✓ → palindrome ✓
        Add [0,2] → result = [[0,2]]
Step 5: i=1, j=0: candidate = "b" + "a" = "ba"
        Check: 'b' vs 'a' ✗ → not palindrome
Step 6: i=1, j=1: skip
Step 7: i=1, j=2: candidate = "b" + "a" = "ba"
        Check: 'b' vs 'a' ✗ → not palindrome
Step 8: i=2, j=0: candidate = "a" + "a" = "aa"
        Check: 'a' vs 'a' ✓ → palindrome ✓
        Add [2,0] → result = [[0,2], [2,0]]
Step 9: i=2, j=1: candidate = "a" + "b" = "ab"
        Check: 'a' vs 'b' ✗ → not palindrome
Step 10: i=2, j=2: skip
Final result: [[0,2], [2,0]]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n² * k)
approaches.summary: Check all possible pairs (i,j) by concatenating words[i] and words[j], then verifying if the concatenated string is a palindrome. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Palindrome Check for Concatenated Strings
ladders.desc: Determine if a given string is a palindrome by comparing characters from both ends.
ladders.explanation:
This is the most critical helper function in the brute force approach: checking whether a string is a palindrome. The intuition is that a palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards, so we can verify this by comparing characters symmetrically from the start and end of the string, moving toward the center. This works because if all corresponding character pairs match, the entire string must be symmetric. The algorithm takes a string s as input. We initialize two pointers: left at index 0 (start of string) and right at index len(s)-1 (end of string). While left < right, we compare s[left] and s[right]. If they differ, we immediately return False since the string cannot be a palindrome. If they match, we increment left and decrement right to move toward the center. When left >= right (we've checked all necessary pairs), we return True. The time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the string, since we examine each character at most once. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a constant amount of extra memory. This sub-routine feeds directly into the full problem (L0) as it's called for every candidate concatenated string. Common mistakes include using a reverse-and-compare approach (which uses O(k) extra space), not handling empty strings correctly (empty string is a palindrome), and off-by-one errors in pointer movement. The two-pointer technique is efficient and fundamental.

Pseudocode:
function isPalindrome(s):
    left = 0
    right = length(s) - 1
    while left < right:
        if s[left] != s[right]:
            return False
        left += 1
        right -= 1
    return True

examples.trace:
Step 1: left=0, right=6, s[0]='r', s[6]='r' → match ✓
Step 2: left=1, right=5, s[1]='a', s[5]='a' → match ✓
Step 3: left=2, right=4, s[2]='c', s[4]='c' → match ✓
Step 4: left=3, right=3 → loop ends (left < right is false)
Return: true

Step 1: left=0, right=4, s[0]='h', s[4]='o' → mismatch ✗
Return: false (immediately, no further checks needed)

        'h' → result = "h"

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through String Characters
ladders.desc: Access each character in a string sequentially using indices or iteration.
ladders.explanation:
This level covers the fundamental skill of traversing a string character by character, which is needed for both palindrome checking and string concatenation. The intuition is that strings are essentially arrays/sequences of characters, and we often need to examine or manipulate individual characters to perform operations on the string as a whole. This works because strings in most programming languages are indexed, allowing direct access to each character position. There are two common approaches: using index-based loops or iterator-based loops. For index-based traversal, we use a loop variable (usually i) that goes from 0 to len(s)-1, and access s[i] at each step. For palindrome checking, we might traverse from both ends simultaneously using two indices. The algorithm for simple forward traversal takes a string s as input. We get n = len(s). Then for i from 0 to n-1, we access s[i] to read the character at that position. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing the entire string. Space complexity is O(1) if we just read characters. This feeds into L3 (concatenation) because concatenation requires reading each character from both strings to build the result, and into L1 (palindrome check) which compares characters from both ends. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., going to len(s) instead of len(s)-1), confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing, and not handling empty strings (where len(s)=0 so loop doesn't execute). Mastering this basic operation is essential for any string manipulation task.

Pseudocode:
function traverseString(s):
    n = length(s)
    for i from 0 to n-1:
        character = s[i]
        # Do something with character
# Or using while loop:
    i = 0
    while i < n:
        character = s[i]
        i += 1

examples.trace:
Step 1: n = length("abc") = 3
Step 2: i=0 → s[0] = 'a'
Step 3: i=1 → s[1] = 'b'
Step 4: i=2 → s[2] = 'c'
Step 5: i=3 → loop ends (i < n is false)
Characters accessed: 'a', 'b', 'c' in order

Step 1: n = length("") = 0
Step 2: Loop from i=0 to -1 doesn't execute (0 < 0 is false immediately)
No characters accessed

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Palindrome?
ladders.desc: Understand the definition and examples of palindromic strings.
ladders.explanation:
This is the foundational concept: understanding what a palindrome is. A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards. This means the first character equals the last character, the second character equals the second-to-last character, and so on, all the way to the middle of the string. The intuition comes from symmetry: if you reverse the string, you get exactly the same string. This concept works because it's based on the definition of string equality when reversed. Examples include single-character strings (like "a"), strings with all same characters (like "aaa"), and more complex symmetric strings (like "racecar"). Empty string is typically considered a palindrome because it reads the same forwards and backwards (it's vacuously true). To verify if a string is a palindrome, you can compare it to its reverse, or compare characters pairwise from both ends. This foundational understanding feeds into L4 (string iteration) because checking palindrome requires accessing characters, and ultimately into all higher levels. Common mistakes include thinking palindromes must have odd length (false: "aa" is palindrome), confusing with other patterns like anagrams, and not considering case sensitivity ("Racecar" vs "racecar" may differ depending on problem constraints). For beginners, it's helpful to practice identifying palindromes manually before coding.

No pseudocode at this conceptual level, but the idea is:
A string s is palindrome if:
    s == reverse(s)
Or equivalently:
    For all i from 0 to len(s)-1: s[i] == s[len(s)-1-i]

examples.trace:
Step 1: Write string: r a d a r
Step 2: Compare forwards and backwards:
        Position 0 (r) vs position 4 (r) → match ✓
        Position 1 (a) vs position 3 (a) → match ✓
        Position 2 (d) vs position 2 (d) → match ✓
All comparisons match, so it's a palindrome.

Step 1: Write string: a p p l e
Step 2: Compare forwards and backwards:
        Position 0 (a) vs position 4 (e) → mismatch ✗
Already found mismatch, so not a palindrome (no need to check further).

approaches.name: HashMap with Palindrome Suffix/Prefix O(n * k²)
approaches.summary: Use a hashmap to store words and their indices, then for each word, check all possible splits into prefix and suffix to find complementary words that form a palindrome. This reduces redundant checks by leveraging precomputed palindromic properties.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Palindrome Pairs Using HashMap with Palindrome Suffix/Prefix
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find all index pairs (i,j) where words[i]+words[j] forms a palindrome using O(n*k²) approach.
ladders.explanation:
This approach efficiently finds all palindrome pairs by leveraging a hashmap and palindrome properties. The core intuition is that for any word, we can split it into prefix and suffix. If the prefix is a palindrome, we need a word that's the reverse of the suffix to complete the palindrome. Similarly, if the suffix is a palindrome, we need the reverse of the prefix. By storing all words and their indices in a hashmap, we can quickly look up these complementary words.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Create a hashmap `word_to_index` mapping each word to its index.
2. Initialize result list `pairs`.
3. For each word at index i:
   a. For each split point j from 0 to len(word):
      - Let prefix = word[0:j], suffix = word[j:len(word)]
      - If prefix is palindrome:
          Look up reverse(suffix) in hashmap. If found at index k and k ≠ i, add (i,k) to pairs.
      - If suffix is palindrome:
          Look up reverse(prefix) in hashmap. If found at index k and k ≠ i, add (k,i) to pairs.
4. Handle empty string case: if empty string exists in words, it pairs with every palindrome word.
5. Return pairs.

Time complexity: O(n*k²) where n is number of words, k is average word length. We check O(k) splits per word, each requiring O(k) time to check palindrome and reverse.
Space complexity: O(n) for hashmap storage.

Connection to L1: This solution uses the palindrome checking helper function from L1 extensively.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to handle the case where i=j (same word) - need to check k ≠ i.
2. Missing empty string handling - empty string concatenated with any palindrome forms a palindrome.
3. Not checking both prefix and suffix cases for each split.
4. Not considering reverse lookup for both cases.

Real code pattern:
def palindromePairs(words):
    word_to_index = {word: i for i, word in enumerate(words)}
    result = []
    
    def is_palindrome(s):
        return s == s[::-1]
    
    for i, word in enumerate(words):
        for j in range(len(word) + 1):
            prefix = word[:j]
            suffix = word[j:]
            
            if is_palindrome(prefix):
                rev_suffix = suffix[::-1]
                if rev_suffix in word_to_index:
                    k = word_to_index[rev_suffix]
                    if k != i:
                        result.append([i, k])
            
            if j != len(word) and is_palindrome(suffix):
                rev_prefix = prefix[::-1]
                if rev_prefix in word_to_index:
                    k = word_to_index[rev_prefix]
                    if k != i:
                        result.append([k, i])
    
    return result

examples.trace:
Step 1: Create hashmap {"bat":0, "tab":1, "cat":2}
Step 2: Process word "bat" (index 0):
  Split at j=0: prefix="", suffix="bat"
    prefix is palindrome (empty string)
    rev_suffix = "tab"
    "tab" found at index 1, add [0,1]
  Split at j=1: prefix="b", suffix="at"
    "b" is palindrome
    rev_suffix = "ta" not in hashmap
    "at" not palindrome
  Split at j=2: prefix="ba", suffix="t"
    "ba" not palindrome
    "t" is palindrome
    rev_prefix = "ab" not in hashmap
  Split at j=3: prefix="bat", suffix=""
    "bat" not palindrome
    "" is palindrome
    rev_prefix = "tab" found at index 1, add [1,0]
Step 3: Process word "tab" (index 1):
  Similar process finds [1,0] already added
Step 4: Process word "cat" (index 2):
  No valid pairs found
Result: [[0,1],[1,0]]

Step 1: Create hashmap {"abcd":0, "dcba":1, "lls":2, "s":3, "sssll":4}
Step 2: Process "abcd" (index 0):
  Split at j=0: prefix="", suffix="abcd"
    rev_suffix = "dcba" found at index 1, add [0,1]
  Split at j=4: prefix="abcd", suffix=""
    rev_prefix = "dcba" found at index 1, add [1,0]
Step 3: Process "dcba" (index 1):
  Finds same pairs already added
Step 4: Process "lls" (index 2):
  Split at j=2: prefix="ll", suffix="s"
    "ll" is palindrome
    rev_suffix = "s" found at index 3, add [2,3]
  Split at j=3: prefix="lls", suffix=""
    rev_prefix = "sll" not in hashmap
Step 5: Process "s" (index 3):
  Split at j=0: prefix="", suffix="s"
    rev_suffix = "s" found at index 3 (same index), skip
  Split at j=1: prefix="s", suffix=""
    rev_prefix = "s" found at index 3 (same index), skip
Step 6: Process "sssll" (index 4):
  Split at j=3: prefix="sss", suffix="ll"
    "sss" is palindrome
    rev_suffix = "ll" not in hashmap
    "ll" is palindrome
    rev_prefix = "sss" not in hashmap
  Split at j=5: prefix="sssll", suffix=""
    rev_prefix = "llsss" not in hashmap
  Actually finds [2,4] from different split
Result: [[0,1],[1,0],[3,2],[2,4]]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Palindrome Checking and Word Splitting
ladders.desc: The helper function that checks if a string is palindrome and handles all split positions.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the heart of the palindrome pairs solution. The intuition is that to form a palindrome by concatenating two words, we need to find split points where one part is already a palindrome, and the other part has a reverse match in our word list.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. For a given word, iterate through all possible split positions (from 0 to len(word)).
2. At each split j, create prefix = word[0:j] and suffix = word[j:len(word)].
3. Check if prefix is palindrome using two-pointer technique.
4. If yes, the reverse of suffix needs to exist in our word list.
5. Check if suffix is palindrome.
6. If yes, the reverse of prefix needs to exist in our word list.
7. Return all valid pairs found for this word.

Time complexity: O(k²) per word where k is word length. We have O(k) splits, each requiring O(k) time for palindrome check.
Space complexity: O(1) additional space.

Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called for each word in the main solution to find all possible pairs involving that word.

Common mistakes:
1. Not checking all split positions (including 0 and len(word)).
2. Forgetting to check both prefix and suffix cases.
3. Inefficient palindrome checking that takes O(k²) instead of O(k).
4. Not handling empty string cases properly.

Real code pattern:
def find_pairs_for_word(word, index, word_to_index):
    result = []
    n = len(word)
    
    for j in range(n + 1):
        prefix = word[:j]
        suffix = word[j:]
        
        # Check if prefix is palindrome
        if prefix == prefix[::-1]:
            rev_suffix = suffix[::-1]
            if rev_suffix in word_to_index:
                k = word_to_index[rev_suffix]
                if k != index:
                    result.append([index, k])
        
        # Check if suffix is palindrome (avoid duplicate when j=0)
        if j != n and suffix == suffix[::-1]:
            rev_prefix = prefix[::-1]
            if rev_prefix in word_to_index:
                k = word_to_index[rev_prefix]
                if k != index:
                    result.append([k, index])
    
    return result

examples.trace:
Word length: 3
Split j=0: prefix="", suffix="bat"
  prefix is palindrome (empty string)
  rev_suffix = "tab" found at index 1, add [0,1]
  suffix "bat" not palindrome
Split j=1: prefix="b", suffix="at"
  prefix "b" is palindrome
  rev_suffix = "ta" not in hashmap
  suffix "at" not palindrome
Split j=2: prefix="ba", suffix="t"
  prefix "ba" not palindrome
  suffix "t" is palindrome
  rev_prefix = "ab" not in hashmap
Split j=3: prefix="bat", suffix=""
  prefix "bat" not palindrome
  suffix "" is palindrome
  rev_prefix = "tab" found at index 1, add [1,0]
Result: [[0,1],[1,0]]

Word length: 3
Split j=0: prefix="", suffix="lls"
  prefix is palindrome
  rev_suffix = "sll" not in hashmap
  suffix "lls" not palindrome
Split j=1: prefix="l", suffix="ls"
  prefix "l" is palindrome
  rev_suffix = "sl" not in hashmap
  suffix "ls" not palindrome
Split j=2: prefix="ll", suffix="s"
  prefix "ll" is palindrome
  rev_suffix = "s" found at index 3, add [2,3]
  suffix "s" is palindrome
  rev_prefix = "ll" not in hashmap
Split j=3: prefix="lls", suffix=""
  prefix "lls" not palindrome
  suffix "" is palindrome
  rev_prefix = "sll" not in hashmap
Result: [[2,3]]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Palindrome Property and Reverse Matching
ladders.desc: Understanding how palindrome properties enable efficient pair finding through reverse matching.
ladders.explanation:
The core insight is that for two words A and B to form a palindrome when concatenated as A+B, there must exist a split point in A such that:
1. The prefix of A is a palindrome, and the reverse of the suffix of A equals B, OR
2. The suffix of A is a palindrome, and the reverse of the prefix of A equals B.

This works because in a palindrome, characters mirror around the center. When we concatenate A and B, the joining point creates a natural split. If the prefix of A is already palindrome, then B must be the reverse of A's suffix to complete the palindrome.

Mathematically: If A = P + S where P is prefix, S is suffix:
- If P is palindrome, then for A+B to be palindrome, we need B = reverse(S)
- If S is palindrome, then for A+B to be palindrome, we need B = reverse(P)

Step-by-step reasoning:
1. Consider concatenation A+B.
2. Imagine the palindrome center could be within A, at the junction, or within B.
3. By splitting A at all positions, we cover all possibilities where the center is in A.
4. The reverse matching ensures B provides the mirror characters.

Time analysis: For each word of length k, we check k+1 split points.
Space analysis: We need to store reverse lookups.

Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the split checking routine.

Common mistakes:
1. Not understanding why both prefix and suffix cases are needed.
2. Confusing when to use reverse(prefix) vs reverse(suffix).
3. Missing the case where one word is completely contained within the palindrome.

Real code pattern:
# Core logic for one split position
def check_split(prefix, suffix, word_index, word_to_index):
    pairs = []
    
    # Case 1: prefix is palindrome
    if is_palindrome(prefix):
        target = reverse(suffix)
        if target in word_to_index:
            pairs.append([word_index, word_to_index[target]])
    
    # Case 2: suffix is palindrome
    if is_palindrome(suffix):
        target = reverse(prefix)
        if target in word_to_index:
            pairs.append([word_to_index[target], word_index])
    
    return pairs

examples.input: Word A="bat", considering split at position 0
examples.output: If prefix="" is palindrome, need B=reverse("bat")="tab"
examples.trace:
A = "bat"
Split at j=0: prefix="", suffix="bat"
Condition 1: prefix "" is palindrome (empty string always palindrome)
Therefore need B = reverse(suffix) = reverse("bat") = "tab"
If "tab" exists in word list, then "bat"+"tab" = "battab" which is palindrome
Verification: "battab" reversed is "battab" ✓

examples.input: Word A="lls", considering split at position 2
examples.output: If prefix="ll" is palindrome, need B=reverse("s")="s"
examples.trace:
A = "lls"
Split at j=2: prefix="ll", suffix="s"
Condition 1: prefix "ll" is palindrome ("ll" reversed is "ll")
Therefore need B = reverse(suffix) = reverse("s") = "s"
If "s" exists in word list, then "lls"+"s" = "llss" which is NOT palindrome
Wait, let's check: "llss" reversed is "ssll" ≠ "llss"
Actually "lls"+"s" = "llss"
But the correct concatenation should be "lls" + "s" = "llss"
And "s" + "lls" = "slls" which IS palindrome!
The correct logic: when prefix is palindrome, we put B first: B + A = "s" + "lls" = "slls" ✓

testCases.expected: prefix not palindrome, suffix not palindrome
prefix palindrome (need B="a"), suffix palindrome (need B="a")

ladders.title: Building Block — String Reversal and Hashmap Lookup
ladders.desc: Efficiently reversing strings and performing O(1) lookups using hashmap.
ladders.explanation:
This building block combines two fundamental operations: string reversal and hashmap lookup. The intuition is that by storing all words in a hashmap with their indices, we can instantly check if the reverse of any substring exists in our word list.

String reversal is needed because if we have a palindrome A+B, then B must be the reverse of some part of A (or vice versa). The hashmap allows O(1) average time lookup to check if this reverse exists.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Create a hashmap/dictionary mapping each word to its index.
2. For any string s, compute its reverse using slicing or two-pointer technique.
3. Check if reverse(s) exists in hashmap.
4. If found, retrieve the index.
5. Ensure it's not the same word (i ≠ j).

Time complexity: Reversal takes O(k) where k is string length. Hashmap lookup takes O(1) average.
Space complexity: O(n) for storing n words in hashmap.

Connection to L2: This enables the reverse matching in the core logic - once we identify what reverse string we need, we can quickly check if it exists.

Common mistakes:
1. Not handling duplicate words correctly (need to store indices properly).
2. Inefficient string reversal that creates many intermediate strings.
3. Forgetting that hashmap keys must be unique (handle duplicates by storing first occurrence or list of indices).
4. Case sensitivity issues.

Real code pattern:
def build_word_map(words):
    """Build hashmap from words to indices"""
    word_to_index = {}
    for i, word in enumerate(words):
        # Store only first occurrence if duplicates exist
        if word not in word_to_index:
            word_to_index[word] = i
    return word_to_index

def reverse_string(s):
    """Return reverse of string s"""
    return s[::-1]  # Python slicing

def find_reverse_in_map(s, word_map):
    """Check if reverse of s exists in map, return index or -1"""
    rev = reverse_string(s)
    return word_map.get(rev, -1)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Build hashmap:
  word_to_index = {"bat":0, "tab":1, "cat":2}
Step 2: String to check: "bat"
Step 3: Reverse "bat" = "tab"
Step 4: Lookup "tab" in hashmap
Step 5: Found at index 1
Result: Index 1

Step 1: Build hashmap:
  word_to_index = {"abcd":0, "dcba":1, "lls":2, "s":3}
Step 2: String to check: "ll"
Step 3: Reverse "ll" = "ll"
Step 4: Lookup "ll" in hashmap
Step 5: "ll" not found
Result: -1 (not found)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Palindrome Checking Algorithm
ladders.desc: Determining if a given string reads the same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation:
A palindrome is a string that reads the same forwards and backwards. This operation is fundamental to the palindrome pairs problem because we need to check if prefixes and suffixes are palindromes.

The intuition is simple: compare the string with its reverse. If they're identical, it's a palindrome. Alternatively, use two pointers starting from both ends moving toward the center, comparing characters at each step.

Step-by-step algorithm (two-pointer approach):
1. Initialize left = 0, right = len(s)-1
2. While left < right:
   a. If s[left] ≠ s[right], return False
   b. left += 1, right -= 1
3. Return True

For empty string: By definition, empty string is considered a palindrome.
For single character: Always a palindrome.

Time complexity: O(k) where k is string length. Each character is compared at most once.
Space complexity: O(1) as we only use pointers.

Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly when checking if prefixes and suffixes are palindromes.

Common mistakes:
1. Not handling empty string case.
2. Off-by-one errors in pointer movement.
3. Case sensitivity issues ("Racecar" vs "racecar").
4. Including spaces or punctuation (problem states words only).

Real code pattern:
def is_palindrome(s):
    """Check if string s is palindrome"""
    # Two-pointer approach
    left, right = 0, len(s) - 1
    while left < right:
        if s[left] != s[right]:
            return False
        left += 1
        right -= 1
    return True

# Alternative using reverse
def is_palindrome_simple(s):
    return s == s[::-1]

examples.trace:
Step 1: left=0 ("r"), right=6 ("r") - equal ✓
Step 2: left=1 ("a"), right=5 ("a") - equal ✓
Step 3: left=2 ("c"), right=4 ("c") - equal ✓
Step 4: left=3 ("e"), right=3 - loop ends
Result: True

Step 1: left=0 ("h"), right=4 ("o") - not equal ✗
Result: False (immediate return)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Manipulation Basics
ladders.desc: Fundamental string operations: accessing characters, slicing, and concatenation.
ladders.explanation:
Strings are sequences of characters. Understanding basic string operations is essential for working with the palindrome pairs problem. The key operations needed are:

1. Accessing characters by index: s[i] returns character at position i (0-based)
2. Slicing: s[start:end] returns substring from start to end-1
3. Length: len(s) returns number of characters
4. Concatenation: s1 + s2 joins two strings
5. Empty string: "" is a valid string with length 0

Intuition: In the palindrome pairs problem, we need to split words into prefixes and suffixes, reverse substrings, and concatenate words. All these rely on these basic operations.

Step-by-step examples:
- For word "hello":
  s[0] = 'h', s[1] = 'e', s[4] = 'o'
  s[0:2] = "he" (characters at indices 0,1)
  s[2:5] = "llo" (indices 2,3,4)
  len(s) = 5
  "hello" + "world" = "helloworld"

Time complexity: Access and length are O(1). Slicing is O(k) where k is slice length.
Space complexity: Creating new strings uses additional memory.

Connection to L4: Palindrome checking uses character access and comparison.

Common beginner mistakes:
1. Forgetting that string indices start at 0.
2. Confusing s[i:j] includes i but excludes j.
3. Trying to modify strings directly (strings are immutable).
4. Not understanding empty string behavior.

Real code pattern:
# Basic string operations
def string_basics_example():
    s = "hello"
    
    # Access
    first_char = s[0]  # 'h'
    last_char = s[-1]  # 'o' (negative indexing)
    
    # Slicing
    prefix = s[0:2]  # "he"
    suffix = s[2:]   # "llo" (from index 2 to end)
    
    # Length
    length = len(s)  # 5
    
    # Concatenation
    combined = s + " world"  # "hello world"
    
    # Empty string
    empty = ""
    empty_length = len(empty)  # 0

examples.input: String: "algorithm", Get prefix of length 3 and suffix from position 3
examples.trace:
String: "algorithm"
Indices: 0:a, 1:l, 2:g, 3:o, 4:r, 5:i, 6:t, 7:h, 8:m
Step 1: Prefix of length 3 = s[0:3]
  Start at index 0, end at index 3 (exclusive)
  Includes indices 0,1,2 → "alg"
Step 2: Suffix from position 3 = s[3:]
  Start at index 3, go to end
  Includes indices 3,4,5,6,7,8 → "orithm"
Result: prefix="alg", suffix="orithm"

String: "race"
Indices: 0:r, 1:a, 2:c, 3:e
Step 1: s[::-1] means:
  Start: end of string
  End: beginning of string
  Step: -1 (go backwards)
Step 2: Process:
  Index 3: 'e'
  Index 2: 'c'
  Index 1: 'a'
  Index 0: 'r'
Result: "ecar"

approaches.name: Trie with Palindrome Checking O(n * k²)
approaches.summary: Build a trie from the words, then for each word, traverse the trie in reverse to find pairs where the concatenation is a palindrome, checking palindromic suffixes during traversal. This optimizes prefix matching and reduces space compared to hashmaps in some cases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find All Palindrome Pairs Using Trie with Palindrome Checking
ladders.desc: Complete solution using trie to efficiently find all pairs (i,j) where words[i]+words[j] is a palindrome.
ladders.explanation: This solution builds a trie from all words, then for each word, traverses the trie in reverse to find matching pairs. The key insight is that when concatenating word A with word B to form a palindrome, there are three cases: 1) B is a complete reverse of A, 2) A is longer than B and the suffix of A is a palindrome, 3) B is longer than A and the prefix of B is a palindrome. We handle all cases by inserting each word into the trie normally, then for each word, we traverse the trie in reverse while checking palindromic suffixes. The trie stores at each node the indices of words that have palindromic suffixes from that point onward. This allows O(n*k²) time complexity where n is number of words and k is average word length. The intuition is that we're matching prefixes efficiently while checking palindromic properties of the remaining parts. Step-by-step: 1) Build trie with all words, storing at each node indices of words with palindromic suffixes from that node. 2) For each word, traverse the trie in reverse character by character. 3) When we reach a node marking a complete word, check if the remaining part of our current word is a palindrome. 4) Also check nodes where we have palindromic suffix markers. 5) Add valid pairs to result. Time: O(n*k²) for building trie (n*k nodes) and checking palindromes (k² per word). Space: O(n*k) for trie storage. This connects to L1 by using the palindrome checking and trie traversal as subroutines. Common mistakes: forgetting to check both directions (i,j and j,i), not handling empty strings properly, missing cases where one word is completely contained in another. Real code pattern: class TrieNode with children dict, word_end index, and palindrome_suffixes list.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build trie. Insert 'bat' (index 0): b->a->t, mark node t with word_end=0. Check suffixes: 'at' not palindrome, 't' palindrome, so add index 0 to palindrome_suffixes at node a. Insert 'tab' (index 1): t->a->b, mark node b with word_end=1. Suffixes: 'ab' not palindrome, 'b' palindrome, add index 1 to palindrome_suffixes at node a. Insert 'cat' (index 2): c->a->t, mark node t with word_end=2. Suffixes: 'at' not palindrome, 't' palindrome, add index 2 to palindrome_suffixes at node a. Step 2: Process each word. For 'bat' (index 0): traverse reverse 't','a','b'. At node t: not word_end. At node a: check palindrome_suffixes [0,1,2] but skip 0 (same word). Check remaining 'b' (from 'bat' after 'ta'): 'b' is palindrome, so pair (0,1) valid. Continue to node b: word_end=1, remaining '' is palindrome, so pair (0,1) again (duplicate but we track). For 'tab' (index 1): similar gives (1,0). For 'cat': no pairs. Final: [(0,1),(1,0)].

Step 1: Build trie. Insert 'a' (index 0): node a word_end=0, suffixes: '' palindrome, add index 0 to palindrome_suffixes at root. Insert 'b' (index 1): node b word_end=1, suffixes: '' palindrome, add index 1 to palindrome_suffixes at root. Insert 'c' (index 2): similar. Insert 'aa' (index 3): a->a, second a node word_end=3, first a has palindrome_suffixes with index 3. Step 2: Process. For 'a' (index 0): traverse trie in reverse 'a'. At node a: word_end=3 (not same index), remaining '' palindrome, pair (0,3). For 'aa' (index 3): traverse reverse 'a','a'. At first a: word_end=0, remaining 'a' palindrome, pair (3,0). At second a: word_end=3 (same index, skip). Others: no pairs. Final: [(0,3),(3,0)].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Trie Traversal with Palindrome Suffix Checking
ladders.desc: For a given word, traverse the trie in reverse while checking palindromic suffixes to find matching pairs.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the solution where we take a single word and find all other words that can combine with it to form a palindrome. The intuition is that we're matching the current word in reverse against prefixes stored in the trie. As we traverse character by character backwards through the word, we move through the trie. At each node we visit, we check two things: 1) If this node marks the end of a word, we check if the remaining part of our current word (the part we haven't traversed yet) is a palindrome. If yes, we found a pair. 2) We check all word indices stored in this node's palindrome_suffixes list - these are words that have palindromic suffixes from this point, meaning if we append our remaining part (which would complete the word), the combination could be palindrome. The algorithm: function find_pairs_for_word(word, index, trie_root): result = []; node = trie_root; for i from len(word)-1 down to 0: char = word[i]; if node has child for char: node = node.children[char]; if node.word_end != -1 and i > 0 and is_palindrome(word[0:i]): result.append((index, node.word_end)); for pal_idx in node.palindrome_suffixes: if pal_idx != index: result.append((index, pal_idx)); else: break; After loop, also check if we reached end of word and node.word_end valid. Time: O(k²) for one word where k is length, due to palindrome checks. Space: O(1) extra. This feeds into L0 by being called for each word. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in palindrome checking bounds, forgetting to skip same word index, not handling case where word is shorter than trie path. Real code pattern: while loop through word reversed, with early break if char not in children.

examples.trace: Trie has path: t->a->b with word_end=1 at node b. Start at root. i=2: char='t', move to node t. i=1: char='a', move to node a. Check node a: word_end=-1, palindrome_suffixes empty. i=0: char='b', move to node b. Check node b: word_end=1, remaining part of word is '' (since i=0), '' is palindrome, so add (0,1). Done.

examples.input: Word: 'lls', index: 2, Trie: contains 's' at node s
examples.trace: Trie has 's' with word_end=3 at node s. Start root. i=2: char='s', move to node s. Check node s: word_end=3, remaining part is 'll' (word[0:2]), 'll' is palindrome, so add (2,3). Continue: i=1: char='l', node s has no child 'l', break. Done.

testCases.input: Word: '', index: 1, Trie: has 'a' at node a

ladders.title: Core Logic — Matching Reverse Prefixes with Palindrome Suffix Verification
ladders.desc: The central technique of checking if word A + word B is palindrome by matching A's reverse against B's prefix and verifying remaining part.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental insight that makes the trie approach work. For two words A and B to form a palindrome when concatenated as A+B, we need A's reverse to match a prefix of B, and then the remaining part of B (after the matched prefix) must be a palindrome. Alternatively, if B is shorter, then A's reverse matches all of B and the remaining part of A must be palindrome. Formally: Let A be word[i], B be word[j]. We want A+B to be palindrome. This means reverse(A) should equal some prefix of B, and the suffix of B (after this prefix) must be palindrome. Or if reverse(A) equals entire B, then nothing remains. The trie helps efficiently check this by storing all words' prefixes. When we process A, we traverse the trie with reverse(A). If we reach a node marking end of some word B, then reverse(A) matched entire B, so we check if remaining part of A (which is empty) is palindrome - always true, so pair valid. If we stop earlier at some node, then reverse(A) matched a prefix of B, and we need to check if the remaining part of B (from that node onward) is palindrome - this is why we store palindrome_suffixes at nodes. The intuition is breaking the palindrome condition into two parts: prefix matching and palindrome checking of the remainder. This reduces O(n²) brute force to O(n*k²). Time analysis: For each pair consideration, we do O(k) traversal and O(k) palindrome check, but aggregated efficiently. Space: O(1) for this logic. Connects to L1 by being implemented in the traversal function. Common mistakes: confusing when to check A's remainder vs B's remainder, not considering case where words are equal length. Real code pattern: if node.word_end valid: check palindrome of word[0:current_index]; also check node.palindrome_suffixes.

examples.trace: Check A+B='battab'. Reverse(A)='tab'. Does 'tab' match prefix of B? B='tab', yes exactly matches entire B. Remaining part of B is ''. Is '' palindrome? Yes. So valid.

Check A+B='llss'. Reverse(A)='sll'. Does 's' match prefix of B? B='s', yes matches first char 's'. Remaining part of B after 's' is ''. Is '' palindrome? Yes. But wait, we used only first char of reverse(A). Actually, for this to work, we need reverse(A) to match entire B, but here reverse(A)='sll' only first char 's' matches B='s'. So we consider: reverse(A) matched prefix 's' of B, and remaining part of B is '' (palindrome), so valid. This is the case where B is shorter.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Construction with Palindrome Suffix Storage
ladders.desc: Build a trie from words while storing at each node indices of words that have palindromic suffixes from that node.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves creating the trie data structure that enables efficient prefix matching. A trie (prefix tree) is built where each node represents a character, and paths from root to nodes represent prefixes of words. For this problem, we enhance the trie by storing two pieces of information at each node: 1) word_end: the index of a word that ends at this node (or -1 if none). 2) palindrome_suffixes: a list of word indices where, from this node onward to the end of the word, the remaining suffix is a palindrome. The intuition for storing palindrome_suffixes is that when we're traversing the trie with a word in reverse, if we stop at a node (meaning we've matched a prefix), we need to know if the remaining part of the word in the trie (from this node to its end) is palindrome. By precomputing and storing this information during insertion, we avoid repeated palindrome checks during query time. Algorithm: class TrieNode with children dict, word_end=-1, palindrome_suffixes list. Insert word with index: node = root; for each char in word: if char not in node.children, create new node; node = node.children[char]; After inserting all chars, set node.word_end = index. Then, to populate palindrome_suffixes: traverse from end back to start, at each node check if remaining suffix is palindrome, if yes add index to node.palindrome_suffixes. Time: O(k²) per word for palindrome checks during insertion. Space: O(n*k) for trie. Connects to L2 by providing the data structure for prefix matching. Common mistakes: forgetting to reset word_end for different words ending at same node, incorrect palindrome checking bounds. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(word)): check if word[i:] is palindrome, if yes add to current node.palindrome_suffixes.

examples.output: Trie with nodes b,a,t; palindrome_suffixes at node a contains 0
examples.trace: Start at root. char 'b': create node b, move to b. Check suffix 'at' from current node? Actually we check from next position: at node b, remaining word is 'at', not palindrome. char 'a': create node a under b, move to a. Check suffix 't' from node a, 't' is palindrome, so add index 0 to node a.palindrome_suffixes. char 't': create node t under a, move to t. Set node t.word_end=0. Check suffix '' from node t, '' is palindrome, add index 0 to node t.palindrome_suffixes. Done.

examples.output: Trie extends with t,a,b; palindrome_suffixes updated
examples.trace: Root. char 't': create node t, move to t. Check suffix 'ab' not palindrome. char 'a': create node a under t, move to a. Check suffix 'b' is palindrome, add index 1 to node a.palindrome_suffixes. char 'b': create node b under a, move to b. Set word_end=1. Check suffix '' palindrome, add index 1 to node b.palindrome_suffixes.

testCases.expected: Node a: word_end=0, palindrome_suffixes at root contains 0
Nodes a,a: second a word_end=0, palindrome_suffixes at first a contains 0
Nodes a,b: b word_end=0, palindrome_suffixes at a contains 0
Nodes a,b,c: c word_end=0, palindrome_suffixes at b contains 0
Root: word_end=0, palindrome_suffixes at root contains 0
Nodes r,a,c,e: e word_end=0, palindrome_suffixes at c contains 0
Nodes b,a,t: t word_end=0, palindrome_suffixes at a contains 0
Nodes l,l,s: s word_end=0, palindrome_suffixes at second l contains 0
Nodes t,e,s,t: second t word_end=0, palindrome_suffixes at s contains 0
Nodes a,b,c,d,c,b,a: last a word_end=0, palindrome_suffixes at multiple nodes

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check if a String is Palindrome
ladders.desc: Determine if a given string reads the same forwards and backwards.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks whether a string is a palindrome, which is essential for verifying if word concatenations form palindromes. A palindrome is a string that equals its reverse. The intuition is comparing characters from both ends moving inward. Algorithm: Use two pointers, left at start (index 0) and right at end (index len(s)-1). While left < right, if s[left] != s[right], return false; otherwise increment left and decrement right. If loop completes, return true. For empty string or single character, it's always palindrome. Time: O(k) where k is string length. Space: O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used repeatedly during trie insertion to populate palindrome_suffixes. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors, not handling empty string correctly, using string reversal which is O(k) time but also O(k) space. Real code pattern: def is_palindrome(s): left, right = 0, len(s)-1; while left < right: if s[left] != s[right]: return False; left+=1; right-=1; return True.

examples.trace: left=0 ('r'), right=6 ('r'): equal. left=1 ('a'), right=5 ('a'): equal. left=2 ('c'), right=4 ('c'): equal. left=3 ('e'), right=3: loop ends (left<right false). Return True.
left=0 ('h'), right=4 ('o'): not equal, return False immediately.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Trie (Prefix Tree) Data Structure
ladders.desc: A tree-like data structure that stores strings for efficient prefix searching.
ladders.explanation: A trie, also called a prefix tree, is a fundamental data structure used to store a dynamic set of strings where keys are usually strings. It's a tree where each node represents a single character, and paths from the root to nodes represent prefixes or complete words. The intuition is that shared prefixes among strings are stored only once, saving space and enabling fast prefix-based operations. Each node typically has: 1) children: a mapping from characters to child nodes, 2) is_end: a boolean indicating if a word ends at this node. To insert a word: start at root, for each character in the word, if the character is not in current node's children, create a new node; move to that child node. After processing all characters, mark the final node as end of word. To search a word: traverse similarly, checking if all characters exist and final node is marked as end. Time for insertion/search: O(k) where k is word length. Space: O(n*k) in worst case but less if prefixes shared. This connects to L4 as the foundation for storing words efficiently. Common mistakes: not handling case sensitivity, forgetting to mark end nodes, inefficient child storage (using array vs dict). Real code pattern: class TrieNode: def __init__(self): self.children = {}; self.is_end = False. Insert: node = root; for char in word: if char not in node.children: node.children[char] = TrieNode(); node = node.children[char]; node.is_end = True.

examples.trace: Start empty trie. Insert 'cat': root has no child 'c', create node c. Move to c, no child 'a', create node a under c. Move to a, no child 't', create node t under a. Mark node t as end. Insert 'car': root has child 'c', move to c. c has child 'a', move to a. a has no child 'r', create node r under a. Mark node r as end. Now root->c->a has two children: t and r.
Start at root. char 'c': found, move to c. char 'a': found, move to a. char 't': found, move to t. Check is_end: True, so word exists.
